intro.kicker: Topic 6 of 10 • Chapter: Newton's Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Force is an external push or pull that changes or tends to change the state of rest or motion, direction of motion, or shape/size of a body. Dimension: [MLT⁻²]. SI unit: Newton (N); CGS: dyne; 1 N = 10⁵ dyne. Four fundamental forces in nature: Gravitational, Weak Nuclear, Electromagnetic, Strong Nuclear (order of increasing strength). Common mechanics forces include Weight (W = mg), Normal Reaction, Tension, and Spring Force (F = −Kx). NEET heavily tests force types, units, and basic force mechanics.

expectedQuestions.description: Multiple MCQ types — units, dimensional formula, four fundamental forces (relative strengths), and common forces in mechanics (Normal, Tension, Spring). Frequently combined with FBD problems.

timeRequired.description: Cover effects of force, units/dimensions, four fundamental forces with relative strengths, and common contact forces. Especially focus on Normal + Tension FBD applications.

difficulty.description: Definitions are accessible; the four fundamental forces' relative strengths and the Spring Force sign convention are common NEET trap areas.

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics uses SI units but rarely tests CGS conversion. NEET requires both systems plus gravitational units (kgf, gf). The four fundamental forces ranking is NEET-specific emphasis.

bulletNotes: Four fundamental forces ranking (Gravitational < Weak < Electromagnetic < Strong) is a direct NEET factual question.
Units conversion (1 N = 10⁵ dyne; 1 kgf = 9.8 N) appears in NEET numerical MCQs.
Tension and Normal Reaction FBDs underpin 30%+ of all Newton's Laws numericals.

strategySteps.title: Memorise unit conversions
strategySteps.description: 1 N = 10⁵ dyne, 1 kgf = 9.8 N, 1 gf = 980 dyne — these appear directly in numerical conversion MCQs.

strategySteps.title: Four fundamental forces sequence
strategySteps.description: Gravitational < Weak Nuclear < Electromagnetic < Strong Nuclear. Relative strengths: 1 : 10²⁵ : 10³⁶ : 10³⁸. This ranking is reliably tested.

strategySteps.title: Spring Force sign convention
strategySteps.description: F = −Kx: the negative sign indicates the spring force is always restorative (opposes displacement). NEET trap: missing the negative sign or misidentifying K units (N/m).

cards.title: Force — Complete Notes
cards.description: Effects of force, all unit systems, four fundamental forces, common mechanics forces with FBD diagrams and NEET trap list.

cards.title: Force Types Quick Reference
cards.description: One-page card with unit conversions, fundamental force rankings, and spring/tension/normal force rules.

cards.title: Definition and Effects of Force
cards.coreRevision: Force is an external push or pull that produces or tries to produce motion, stops or changes motion, changes direction of motion, or changes shape of a body.
points: CGS unit: Dyne = 1 g·cm/s²; 1 N = 10⁵ dyne
cards.example: A bat swinging at a ball exerts force that changes the ball's direction and speed simultaneously — two effects of force acting together.

cards.title: Unit Systems — Force
cards.coreRevision: Absolute units: Newton (SI), Dyne (CGS). Gravitational units: kilogram-force, gram-force. Conversion: 1 N = 10⁵ dyne; 1 kgf = 9.8 N.
points: 1 Newton: force giving 1 m/s² to 1 kg mass
1 Dyne: force giving 1 cm/s² to 1 g mass
cards.example: A 70 kg person's weight = 70 × 9.8 = 686 N ≈ 70 kgf. These are equivalent representations.

cards.title: Four Fundamental Forces in Nature
cards.coreRevision: Gravitational : Weak Nuclear : Electromagnetic : Strong Nuclear = 1 : 10²⁵ : 10³⁶ : 10³⁸. Strong Nuclear is the strongest force.
points: Gravitational: weakest — acts between all masses (via Gravitons)
Strong Nuclear: strongest — binds nucleus (via pions)
cards.example: At nuclear distances (~10⁻¹⁵ m), Strong Nuclear >> Electromagnetic >> Weak Nuclear >> Gravitational. At astronomical scales, Gravitational dominates as other forces decay faster.

cards.title: Common Forces in Mechanics
cards.coreRevision: Weight W = mg (gravitational). Normal N: perpendicular to surface. Tension T: along string, away from body. Spring F = −Kx (restoring).
points: Normal force: not always == mg; depends on acceleration and angle
Tension is same throughout a massless inextensible string
Spring constant K has units N/m; F = −Kx (Hooke's Law)
cards.example: In an elevator accelerating upward at a, apparent weight = m(g + a) > mg. The normal force from the floor exceeds the gravitational weight — distinguishing N from W is critical.

curriculum.hintText: Key differences between CBSE/NEET and US AP Physics treatment of Force.

gaps.title: CGS and Gravitational Force Units
gaps.description: US AP Physics uses SI only. NEET tests CGS units (dyne) and gravitational units (kgf, gf).

gaps.title: Four Fundamental Forces Ranking
gaps.description: NEET explicitly tests the relative strength ordering and associated particles. US AP typically does not.
bullets: Order: Gravitational (weakest) < Weak Nuclear < Electromagnetic < Strong Nuclear (strongest)

gaps.title: Conservative vs Non-Conservative Forces
gaps.description: NEET classifies forces — central, conservative, non-conservative. US AP focuses on work-energy theorem without this classification.
bullets: Conservative: gravitational, electric, elastic (work done = 0 in round trip)

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct order of fundamental forces from weakest to strongest?

options.text: Gravitational < Weak Nuclear < Electromagnetic < Strong Nuclear
Strong Nuclear < Electromagnetic < Weak Nuclear < Gravitational
Gravitational < Electromagnetic < Weak Nuclear < Strong Nuclear
Electromagnetic < Gravitational < Weak Nuclear < Strong Nuclear

questions.explanation: The correct order of increasing strength: Gravitational (1) < Weak Nuclear (10²⁵) < Electromagnetic (10³⁶) < Strong Nuclear (10³⁸). This is a direct NCERT factual question tested in NEET.

questions.question: The dimensional formula for force is:
questions.explanation: Force = mass × acceleration = M × LT⁻² = [MLT⁻²]. Note: [MLT⁻¹] is momentum, [ML²T⁻²] is energy/torque.

questions.question: The restoring force of a spring is given by F = −Kx. The SI unit of K is:
questions.explanation: K = F/x; units = N/m. The spring constant measures force per unit extension. It is NOT N·m (which would be torque or energy).

questions.question: 1 Newton is equal to how many dynes?
Which fundamental force is responsible for binding protons and neutrons in the nucleus?
Spring force F = −Kx; the negative sign indicates:
options: Force is in the direction of displacement

questions.question: The ratio of electric force to gravitational force between two electrons is approximately:

items.title: Know force units in all systems
items.description: SI (Newton), CGS (Dyne), Gravitational (kgf, gf). Conversions: 1 N = 10⁵ dyne, 1 kgf = 9.8 N.

items.title: Memorise four fundamental forces order
items.description: Gravitational < Weak Nuclear < Electromagnetic < Strong Nuclear. Strengths: 1 : 10²⁵ : 10³⁶ : 10³⁸.

items.title: Understand Spring Force
items.description: F = −Kx. Negative = restoring. K in N/m. Hooke's Law — valid within elastic limit.

items.title: Distinguish Weight, Normal, Tension
items.description: W = mg (gravity), N = perpendicular contact force (not always = mg), T = along string away from body.

items.title: Conservative vs Non-Conservative forces
items.description: Conservative: gravitational, electric, elastic. Non-conservative: friction, viscous. Central forces are always conservative.

items.question: Why is positive force repulsive and negative force attractive in the convention mentioned?
items.answer: When force is written without explicit direction (as a signed scalar), a positive value means the force pushes objects apart (repulsive — like two similar charges). A negative value means the force pulls objects together (attractive — like opposite charges or gravity). This sign convention encodes direction in a compact form when vector notation is dropped.

items.question: Is tension an electromagnetic force?
items.answer: Yes. Tension in a string is an electromagnetic contact force, arising from intermolecular electrostatic interactions between the atoms of the string. At the macroscopic level it appears as a mechanical force, but its microscopic origin is electromagnetic.

items.question: What is the difference between absolute and gravitational units of force?
items.answer: Absolute units (Newton, dyne) are defined via F = ma and are independent of the local gravitational field. Gravitational units (kgf, gf) are defined as the weight of a standard mass — 1 kgf is the force Earth's gravity exerts on 1 kg (= 9.80 N). Gravitational units change with location; absolute units do not.

grounding_summary: Page 191 defines force as an external push or pull that produces/stops motion, changes direction, or changes shape. It lists all unit systems (Newton, Dyne, kgf, gf) with conversions and the four fundamental forces with relative strengths: Gravitational (1) < Weak Nuclear (10²⁵) < Electromagnetic (10³⁶) < Strong Nuclear (10³⁸).
Page 192 continues with conservative vs non-conservative force classification, and common mechanics forces: Weight (mg), Normal Reaction, Tension (electromagnetic), and Spring Force (F = −Kx).
grounding_excerpt: Force is an external effect in the form of a push or pull which
Negative force - Force between two opposite charges
